summary: DeVault argues that Pine64's funding strategy for its FOSS hardware ecosystem is misallocated, prioritizing distro maintenance over more critical upstream work. He identifies four layers of software needs—kernel/bootloader support, telephony, mobile UI, and distros—and contends Pine64 funds them in reverse order of importance. This creates fragmentation, with 20+ distros each solving the same low-level problems independently instead of one shared upstream solution benefiting everyone. He proposes Pine64 hire a single full-time developer focused on upstreaming kernel patches, bootloader support, and telephony work. He uses the Pinebook Pro as an example where six unmerged patches prevent standard UEFI installation, forcing every distro to publish custom images. While praising Pine64's model, he calls for a strategic course correction.
key_points: Pine64 funds software efforts in reverse priority order: distros get the most while kernel upstreaming and telephony get the least
Twenty-plus distros publishing Pine64-specific images represents massive duplication of effort that upstream patches would eliminate
Distro developers have been forced to become kernel and telephony experts out of necessity, fragmenting their limited volunteer time
A single full-time developer focused on upstreaming would benefit the entire ecosystem more than funding individual distros
The PineNote hardware will suffer even worse from this misallocation without a course change
money_quotes: Pine64 has a really interesting idea: make cheap hardware with low margins, get it into the hands of the FOSS community, and let them come up with the software.
Pine64 themselves contribute no labor to any of these focus areas, and though they provide some funding, they provide it from the bottom of this list up.
Instead of one working solution for everyone, we have 20+ Linux distros publishing Pine64-specific images to flash to microSD cards.
If we instead spent it on the focus areas directly, then Manjaro and all of the other distros would benefit from this work for free.
A working phone with a shitty UI is better than a phone with a pretty UI that doesn't work.

They've become experts with specific allegiances and incentives, and though there's some patch sharing and collaboration between distros, it's done informally across a dozen independent organizational structures.

I think that the best strategic decision would be to hire just one full-time software developer to specifically focus on development and upstreaming in Linux mainline, u-Boot mainline, ModemManager, etc.
Manjaro Linux is a bad Linux distribution and a bad place to send this money.
key_insight: Pine64's funding of downstream distros instead of upstream kernel and telephony work creates ecosystem fragmentation where dozens of projects redundantly solve the same low-level problems that a single upstream contribution would fix for everyone.
